title: Support Vector Machines
summary: Maximum margin classifiers. The kernel trick allows non-linear classification in high dimensions.
key_concepts: Soft margin SVM: C parameter trades margin vs misclassification
Kernel trick: K(x,z) = φ(x)·φ(z) — implicit high-dim mapping
SVMs are convex: guaranteed global optimum
ml_connection: SVMs were SOTA pre-deep learning. Kernel trick appears in Gaussian Processes, kernel PCA. Understanding duality is important for optimization theory.
exercises: Train an SVM with RBF kernel on a non-linearly-separable dataset.
Show how C affects the decision boundary (try C=0.1, 1, 100).
What is the kernel trick and why does it make SVMs tractable?
Compare SVM vs logistic regression on the same dataset.
code_example:
from sklearn.svm import SVC
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
import numpy as np

# SVMs need feature scaling!
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X_train)

# RBF kernel SVM
svm = SVC(kernel='rbf', C=1.0, gamma='scale', probability=True)
svm.fit(X_scaled, y_train)

# Support vectors
print(f'Number of support vectors: {svm.n_support_}')
thinking_questions: Why do SVMs only depend on support vectors (not all training points)?
What is the curse of dimensionality and how does the kernel trick help?
